rename-and-remove-tracked-files.label: Rename and Remove Tracked Files
rename-and-remove-tracked-files.summary: Use Git-aware file operations, inspect the staged result, and record structural changes without losing history.

try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.title: Rename a Tracked Guide
try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.prompt:
Rename `docs/guide-draft.md` to `docs/volunteer-guide.md` with Git, then inspect short status.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.
try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.hint: Read the repository state first, then use the Git command that changes only the requested boundary.

help.concept: git mv changes the working tree path and stages the removal/addition pair as one intentional rename.
help.hint_1: Use the exact file or repository named in the task; avoid broad commands that include unrelated work.
help.hint_2: After the action, run the requested inspection command to confirm the final state.

try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.starterCode:
# Use the terminal for this Git task.
# The required starting files and repository state are already prepared.
try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.solutionCode:
cd release-kit
git mv docs/guide-draft.md docs/volunteer-guide.md
git status --short

main_sh.content:
# Use the terminal for this Git task.
# The required starting files and repository state are already prepared.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf release-kit
mkdir -p release-kit
git -C release-kit init -q -b main
git -C release-kit config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C release-kit config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd release-kit
mkdir -p docs
printf %s '# Draft Guide
' > docs/guide-draft.md
cd ..
cd release-kit
mkdir -p docs
printf %s 'Old checklist
' > docs/old-checklist.md
cd ..
git -C release-kit add -A
git -C release-kit commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'

main_sh.content:
cd release-kit
git mv docs/guide-draft.md docs/volunteer-guide.md
git status --short

help.hint_2: After the action, run the requested inspection command to confirm the final state.

single-choice-1.title: Purpose of git mv
single-choice-1.prompt: What is the primary purpose of the `git mv` command?
single-choice-1.hint: Think about how Git tracks file changes.

help.concept: The `git mv` command is used to rename files while keeping Git informed of the change.
help.hint_1: It combines moving a file and staging the change.
help.hint_2: It ensures the rename is tracked in the next commit.

options.a: To move a file without tracking
options.b: To rename a file and stage the change

single-choice-2.title: Effect of git rm
single-choice-2.prompt: What happens when you use the `git rm` command?
single-choice-2.hint: Consider what happens to the file in the working directory and staging area.

help.concept: The `git rm` command removes a file from both the working directory and the staging area.
help.hint_1: It stages the file for deletion in the next commit.
help.hint_2: The file is removed from the working directory.

options.a: The file is moved to a new location
options.b: The file is removed from the working directory and staged for deletion

multi-choice-1.title: Commands to Verify Staged Changes
multi-choice-1.prompt: Which commands can you use to verify staged changes before committing?
multi-choice-1.hint: Think about commands that show the status and differences of staged changes.

help.concept: Verifying staged changes ensures that only intended modifications are committed.
help.hint_1: Check the status of the working directory and staging area.
help.hint_2: Use commands that show differences between staged changes and the last commit.

drag-reorder-1.title: Steps to Rename and Commit a File
drag-reorder-1.prompt: Arrange the steps to rename a file and commit the change using Git.
drag-reorder-1.hint: Consider the order of operations from renaming to committing.

help.concept: Renaming and committing a file involves several steps to ensure the change is tracked and recorded.
help.hint_1: Start with renaming the file using Git.
help.hint_2: End with committing the change with a message.

tokens.t1: Use git mv to rename the file
tokens.t2: Check the status with git status
tokens.t3: Commit the change with git commit

fill-blank-choice-1.title: Command for Renaming a File
fill-blank-choice-1.prompt: Complete the command to rename `file1.txt` to `file2.txt` using Git.
fill-blank-choice-1.hint: Use git mv followed by the old and new file names.

help.concept: Renaming a file in Git requires using git mv to ensure the change is tracked.
help.hint_1: The command should rename and stage the file in one step.
help.hint_2: Use git mv followed by the old and new file names.

fill-blank-choice-2.title: Command for Removing a File
fill-blank-choice-2.prompt: Complete the command to remove `old_file.txt` using Git.
fill-blank-choice-2.hint: Use git rm followed by the file name.

help.concept: Removing a file in Git requires using git rm to ensure the change is tracked.
help.hint_1: The command should remove and stage the file in one step.
help.hint_2: Use git rm followed by the file name to remove it.

sketch0.title: Rename with Git
sketch0.bodyMarkdown:
Rename a tracked file with `git mv` so the working tree and staging area change together:

```bash
git mv draft-guide.md visitor-guide.md
```

Git records the old path disappearing and the new path appearing. Rename detection is calculated from content, so the staged diff may show a percentage rather than a special stored “rename object.”

Keep the file contents recognizable when the goal is a simple rename.

sketch1.title: Remove with Git
sketch1.bodyMarkdown:
Remove a tracked file from both the working tree and the next snapshot with:

```bash
git rm retired-contact.txt
```

The deletion is staged immediately. The file still exists in earlier commits, so history is preserved.

Use this when the project should no longer contain the path—not merely when you want Git to ignore future edits.

sketch2.title: Verify the Staged Shape
sketch2.bodyMarkdown:
Before committing structural changes, inspect the staged names:

```bash
git status --short
git diff --staged --name-status
```

The name-status view highlights additions, deletions, and detected renames. Confirm the new path exists, the old path is gone, and no unrelated file is staged.

sketch3.title: Commit the Structural Change
sketch3.bodyMarkdown:
Record related rename and removal work in one focused commit when they serve the same cleanup purpose.

A message such as `Organize visitor guide files` explains the result better than `mv and rm files`.

After committing, use status and `git ls-files` to verify the repository tracks the intended final paths.
